caption: Stated 2010 goal: a great Internet movie service combining DVD-by-mail with streaming, growing subscribers and EPS every year. Netflix has since pivoted fully to streaming.
DVD-era subscriber growth: 7.5M (4Q07) to 12.3M (4Q09), the base before the streaming era. Netflix no longer discloses membership counts.
Monthly churn 3.9%-4.6% across 2007-2009, ending 3.9%. Netflix dropped the churn KPI over a decade ago, so this is the only quantified churn baseline in the archive.
Subscriber Acquisition Cost fell from $34.58 (4Q07) to $25.23 (4Q09). Netflix discontinued the metric long ago.
FCF build, 4Q09: OCF $105.8M less $22.4M capex and $57.0M content-library spend = $30.2M FCF. Content was already about half of operating cash in the DVD era.
Summary financials 2004-2009: revenue $501M to $1,670M, operating profit $19M to $192M, EPS $0.33 to $1.98.
WBD deal strategic rationale: Netflix's 190-country reach plus Warner Bros.' library, franchises and HBO Max. Deal was signed Dec 2025 and TERMINATED Feb 27 2026.

WBD transaction summary: $27.75/share ($23.25 cash + $4.50 NFLX stock, collared), cash on hand plus new debt, expected close in 12-18 months. The deal was terminated on 2026-02-27, about 12 weeks after signing.

WBD deal economics: $82.7B EV at 25.2x CY26E EBITDA pre-synergies / 14.3x post-synergies, funded by $50B of new acquisition debt against a balance sheet carrying $5.4B of net debt (0.4x EBITDA). It is the largest leverage signal in Netflix's deck history.
WBD synergy/accretion targets: at least $2-3B run-rate cost savings by year three, GAAP-EPS accretive by year two, pledge to keep investment-grade ratings via rapid debt paydown.
Netflix committed to MAINTAIN Warner Bros.' theatrical film releases and HBO Max & HBO. That reversed its long-standing direct-to-streaming, no-theatrical doctrine.
Business-acceleration thesis: management argued WBD's IP/library would attract and retain subscribers, drive engagement and incremental operating income 'for decades'.

Nielsen distributor-level US TV viewshare, Oct 2025: YouTube 12.9%, Disney 11.4%, NBCUniversal 8.6%, FOX 8.4%, Paramount 8.2%, Netflix 8.0%. It is the only third-party share-of-viewing datapoint in the archive, and it puts Netflix sixth.
